profile.name: Dana Reyes
profile.business_type: Guided outdoor adventure outfitter (rock climbing + backcountry skiing)

goal: Get the legal setup right before next season — several things feel exposed and I'd rather spend money now than after something goes wrong

constraints.budget: $2000 for legal setup if it's one-time; hesitant on ongoing retainers
constraints.team: Solo guide + two seasonal contractors (lead guides for trips I don't personally run)

personality: direct, competent, knows her technical domain but green on the paperwork side, not a catastrophizer
backstory: Fifteen years as a professional guide at larger outfitters, started her own shop two years ago. Technical reputation is strong; she's picky about who she takes out. The legal infrastructure is what she never learned at her prior jobs — the bigger outfitters handled it. This past season she realized she's accumulating exposures: her client waiver is a template she downloaded from a climbing-forum thread; the two lead guides she contracts are technically 1099 but work nearly full-time during season; she's about to add a new service category (avalanche-education classes, which she thinks may need a different instructor certification and liability setup); and she bought a domain and started ordering packaging with the business name without doing a trademark search. Nothing has gone wrong yet. She wants to close these gaps before the next season and before the next hire.
r3_wrong_claim: Okay — I'll just find a better waiver template online and use that. Every outfitter uses templates. That fixes most of it, right?
r4_contradiction: Actually — I should mention something I didn't say earlier. The two 'contractors' I use? They've both been working for me exclusively for a year and a half, I schedule their trips, I set their rates, I pay them per trip. One of them asked me last month about whether they should get workers' comp through me. I didn't know what to tell them. So the 1099 thing might be less settled than I made it sound.
conversational_behaviors: Names multiple exposures at once in the opener rather than one at a time
Asks for urgency ranking — wants to know which to do first, not a list
Will push back if legal advice feels alarmist without reason; values calibration

expected_answer_style.opening: States the business name, location, and 4-5 specific legal gaps she's aware of (waiver, contractor classification, new service category, trademark) in the first message, with the framing 'what do I handle first and how'
expected_answer_style.q2_primary_challenge: The waiver question + the classification question are the biggest two; she'll ask which is most urgent and what kind of lawyer she needs for each.
expected_answer_style.followup: Will respond to specific urgency calibration ('before next season' vs 'before the next hire' vs 'before signing') with appreciation; will reject generic 'consult a lawyer' without category specificity.
